failure_modes.text: Teams can neglect emerging products, markets, or drivers by optimizing only around the current high-impact few.
failure_modes.source_quote: A common anti-pattern is focusing solely on the 20% of products that generate 80% of the revenue, which can lead to neglecting emerging products or markets that could be crucial for future growth.
failure_modes.mitigation: Schedule a periodic scan for emerging drivers: review whether new products, markets, or inputs have crossed the threshold into the vital few since the last assessment.

failure_modes.text: Pareto can become a lazy excuse when people invoke 80/20 language without actually measuring leverage.
failure_modes.source_quote: Danger When the Vital Few Become a Lazy Excuse: The model is distorted when teams invoke 80/20 rhetoric without actually measuring leverage.
failure_modes.mitigation: Require measurement behind every 80/20 claim: do not accept Pareto language without data showing which inputs actually drive disproportionate output.

failure_modes.text: Premature pruning can discard lower-signal branches before they have a fair chance to prove future importance.
failure_modes.source_quote: Prematurely trimming ideas based on early, low-impact metrics can lead to abandoning breakthrough concepts before they have a chance to prove their value.
failure_modes.mitigation: Protect experimental branches with a small dedicated budget so promising long-tail options get a fair trial before being pruned by current-state concentration analysis.

premortem_questions.text: How do you prioritize what to do and what not to do?
premortem_questions.source_quote: How do you prioritize what to do and what not to do?

premortem_questions.text: What is the low-impact 80% that can be minimized, streamlined, or eliminated?
premortem_questions.source_quote: What is the low-impact 80% that can be minimized, streamlined, or eliminated to gain greater clarity and freedom?

premortem_questions.text: Are we trimming based on likelihood of impact, or because some areas are simply easier or less emotionally taxing to ignore?
premortem_questions.source_quote: Are we trimming based on likelihood of impact, or because some areas are simply easier or less emotionally taxing to ignore?

heuristics.text: Search for the vital few by pruning low-impact branches so scarce attention goes to the biggest drivers.
heuristics.source_quote: When a problem is disaggregated into many branches of potential causes or solutions, the 80/20 Rule dictates cutting off the low-impact branches.

heuristics.text: Treat the current leverage pattern as revisitable rather than permanent so the focus can change when the distribution shifts.
heuristics.source_quote: Best when prioritization can be revisited as the distribution shifts.

heuristics.text: Measure impact before invoking Pareto language so the model sharpens focus instead of rationalizing convenience.
heuristics.source_quote: The model is distorted when teams invoke 80/20 rhetoric without actually measuring leverage.

curation_notes.summary: The source is naturally chunk-ready on vital-few prioritization, anti-lazy-pruning discipline, and explicit leverage questions.
donor_drops: Did not preserve donor productivity aphorisms that widened past the raw source's stronger pruning and measurement discipline.
open_questions: The first heuristic compresses both issue-tree pruning and broad resource-allocation examples into one reusable operational move.

allies: The raw allies section is strong on prioritization, leverage, simplification, and bounded rationality for later relation preservation.
antagonists: The raw antagonists section is strong on boiling the ocean and confirmation-bias-adjacent selection problems.
structured_tensions: The source includes an explicit structured tension block that cleanly maps into the current relation layer vocabulary.
